title: Boiled fruit cake
subtitle: British fruit cake made by simmering the fruit first
excerpt: A dense, moist fruit cake where the dried fruit is simmered with butter, sugar, and water before the flour and eggs go in. The boiling method gives a deeply flavoured, moist crumb without creaming or soaking overnight. Keeps for two weeks wrapped in foil.
sourceNotes: The boiled cake method is documented in British domestic cooking from the mid-nineteenth century as a reliable method for producing a moist fruit cake without the lengthy creaming and soaking required by traditional fruit cakes. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: the chapter on plain fruit cakes documents a method of simmering fruit with butter and sugar before adding flour and eggs, noting that the boiling softens the fruit and distributes the fat evenly through the batter. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg: the boiled plum cake is documented as a simpler method than the creamed fruit cake for producing a close-crumbed, moist result.

recipe.freezeNotes: Wrap in foil and freeze. Defrost at room temperature overnight.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Improves significantly over 3–5 days as the fruit moisture distributes through the crumb. Keeps wrapped at room temperature for 2 weeks.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, middle shelf. Cover the top with baking paper after 50 minutes if browning. Internal temperature should reach 92°C

glossaryTerms.definition: A cake-making technique where the fruit, fat, and sugar are simmered together in a saucepan before the dry ingredients and eggs are added. The simmering softens and plumps the fruit, melts the butter into the fruit mixture, and begins to break down the sugars. The mixture must cool before the eggs are added to prevent scrambling. The method produces a reliably moist cake without overnight soaking or lengthy creaming.

content.text: The boiled cake method is exactly what it says: you put the fruit, butter, sugar, and water in a saucepan and bring it to a simmer. The fruit plumps and softens in the liquid; the butter melts through the mix. Once it cools to below 60°C you add the eggs and flour and it goes in the tin. No creaming, no soaking overnight, no complicated method — and the result is a deeply moist, flavourful fruit cake that keeps for two weeks.

Combine the {{raisins}}, {{sultanas}}, {{dried-currants}}, {{mixed-peel}}, {{unsalted-butter}}, {{dark-brown-sugar}}, {{water}}, and {{mixed-spice}} in a medium saucepan. Bring to a gentle simmer over medium heat, stirring until the butter melts and the sugar dissolves. Simmer for 5 minutes. This is the
: the fruit absorbs the liquid and plumps. Pour into a large bowl and leave to cool for 20 minutes.
Add the dry ingredients and eggs

Preheat the oven to 160°C fan. Butter and double-line the round tin with baking paper. Once the fruit mixture has cooled to warm (not hot), stir in the beaten {{eggs}}. Add the {{plain-flour}}, {{bicarbonate-of-soda}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} and fold until fully combined.

Spoon into the prepared tin and level the surface. Bake for 75–80 minutes until a skewer inserted into the centre comes out clean and the internal temperature reads 92°C. Lay a piece of baking paper over the top after 50 minutes if the surface is browning. Leave to cool completely in the tin before turning out. Wrap in foil and store at room temperature.
